author: Augustine of Hippo
note: The famous opening of the Confessions — the restless heart made for God. DELICACY/ACCURACY: the popular English form 'Thou hast made us for Thyself, and our heart is restless until it rests (repose) in Thee' is a paraphrase (and close to Pusey's 1838 wording); the actual NPNF1 Pilkington text reads 'Thou hast formed us for Thyself, and our hearts are restless till they find rest in Thee' — quoted verbatim here. Canonical CCEL locus: NPNF1-01 (npnf101), Confessions Bk I ch. 1; verbatim captured from the parallel static NPNF Pilkington page on Wikisource (CCEL leaf pages are JS-rendered and could not be read verbatim by the fetch tool). Em-dash/comma punctuation around 'resistest the proud' to be confirmed at verification. verified verbatim against the NPNF text (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: Great art Thou, O Lord, and greatly to be praised; great is Thy power, and of Thy wisdom there is no end. And man, being a part of Thy creation, desires to praise Thee, man, who bears about with him his mortality, the witness of his sin, even the witness that Thou 'resistest the proud,'—yet man, this part of Thy creation, desires to praise Thee. Thou movest us to delight in praising Thee; for Thou hast formed us for Thyself, and our hearts are restless till they find rest in Thee.

author: Augustine of Hippo
note: The 'Too late loved I Thee' passage — the soul's discovery that God was within all along. ACCURACY: the widely quoted 'O Thou Beauty of ancient days, yet ever new' is PUSEY's rendering, NOT this NPNF text; the NPNF1 Pilkington translation reads 'O Fairness, so ancient, and yet so new' (Latin: 'pulchritudo tam antiqua et tam nova') — quoted verbatim here. Canonical CCEL locus: NPNF1-01 (npnf101), Confessions Bk X ch. 27; verbatim captured from the parallel static NPNF Pilkington page on Wikisource (CCEL leaf pages are JS-rendered). verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: Too late did I love Thee, O Fairness, so ancient, and yet so new! Too late did I love Thee! For behold, Thou wert within, and I without, and there did I seek Thee; I, unlovely, rushed heedlessly among the things of beauty Thou madest. Thou wert with me, but I was not with Thee. Those things kept me far from Thee, which, unless they were in Thee, were not. Thou calledst, and criedst aloud, and forcedst open my deafness. Thou didst gleam and shine, and chase away my blindness. Thou didst exhale odours, and I drew in my breath and do pant after Thee. I tasted, and do hunger and thirst. Thou didst touch me, and I burned for Thy peace.

author: Augustine of Hippo
note: Augustine's summary of the catholic faith of the Trinity — one substance, indivisible equality, one God, the persons distinct (the Father begets, the Son is begotten, the Spirit is Spirit of both), the Holy Spirit 'co-equal with the Father and the Son.' A clean anti-Arian, anti-modalist statement; safe for denominational neutrality (held in common by Catholics, Orthodox, and Protestants). Text is an excerpt from the single long sentence of I.4.7 (begins mid-sentence after 'have purposed to teach...'); each clause verbatim-confirmed from the static NPNF Haddan page on Wikisource. Canonical CCEL locus: NPNF1-03 (npnf103). verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Spirit intimate a divine unity of one and the same substance in an indivisible equality; and therefore that they are not three Gods, but one God: although the Father hath begotten the Son, and so He who is the Father is not the Son; and the Son is begotten by the Father, and so He who is the Son is not the Father; and the Holy Spirit is neither the Father nor the Son, but only the Spirit of the Father and of the Son, Himself also co-equal with the Father and the Son, and pertaining to the unity of the Trinity.

author: Augustine of Hippo
note: The thesis of The City of God — the two cities distinguished not by geography but by two loves: love of self to the contempt of God (earthly city) versus love of God to the contempt of self (heavenly city). About the orientation of the heart, not double predestination — no reprobation language in the excerpt, so denominationally neutral. Excerpt is the opening of ch. 28 (first three sentences), each verbatim-confirmed from the static NPNF Dods page on Wikisource. Canonical CCEL locus: NPNF1-02 (npnf102). verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: Accordingly, two cities have been formed by two loves: the earthly by the love of self, even to the contempt of God; the heavenly by the love of God, even to the contempt of self. The former, in a word, glories in itself, the latter in the Lord. For the one seeks glory from men; but the greatest glory of the other is God, the witness of conscience.

author: Augustine of Hippo
note: The closing of The City of God — the eternal Sabbath 'consecrated by the resurrection of Christ,' the repose 'not only of the spirit, but also of the body' (bodily resurrection hope), ending in the beloved line 'There we shall rest and see, see and love, love and praise.' Both sentences verbatim-confirmed from the static NPNF Dods page on Wikisource. Canonical CCEL locus: NPNF1-02 (npnf102). verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: But there is not now space to treat of these ages; suffice it to say that the seventh shall be our Sabbath, which shall be brought to a close, not by an evening, but by the Lord's day, as an eighth and eternal day, consecrated by the resurrection of Christ, and prefiguring the eternal repose not only of the spirit, but also of the body. There we shall rest and see, see and love, love and praise. This is what shall be in the end without end.

author: Augustine of Hippo
work: Tractates on the Gospel of John
note: Augustine's 'crede ut intelligas' — 'believe that thou mayest understand.' In §6 this line is introduced by 'For understanding is the reward of faith' (context, not quoted here) and grounded on Isaiah 7:9 LXX ('except ye believe, ye shall not understand'). The load-bearing apologetics locus for 'faith seeking understanding' (later Anselm's 'fides quaerens intellectum'). Sentence verbatim-confirmed from the static NPNF Gibb page on Wikisource (page confirmed as 'Tractate XXIX. Chapter VII. 14-18'). Canonical CCEL locus: NPNF1-07 (npnf107). NOTE: this is NPNF vol. 7, outside the vols 1-3 the brief named, but within the task's allowance for a PD NPNF tractate. verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: Therefore do not seek to understand in order to believe, but believe that thou mayest understand; since, 'except ye believe, ye shall not understand.'
